title: Decompensated Cirrhosis with Tense ASCITES and New SPONTANEOUS BACTERIAL PERITONITIS (SBP) in a 58-Year-Old Man with Alcohol-Related Cirrhosis Admitted for Worsening Abdominal Distension, Low-Grade Fever, Diffuse Abdominal Tenderness, and Early Hepatic Encephalopathy: DIAGNOSTIC Paracentesis BEFORE Antibiotics (Ascitic PMN >= 250 = SBP), Empiric Cefotaxime PLUS IV Albumin to Prevent Hepatorenal Syndrome, Sodium-Restricted Diet with Spironolactone + Furosemide and Daily Weights, Lactulose Titrated to 2-3 Soft Stools for Encephalopathy, ADEQUATE Protein (NOT Protein Restriction), and Avoidance of NSAIDs/Nephrotoxins/Sedatives
subtitle: ASCITES is the most common complication of cirrhosis and signals DECOMPENSATION. The pathophysiologic chain: cirrhosis -> PORTAL HYPERTENSION -> splanchnic arterial VASODILATION (nitric-oxide mediated) -> reduced effective arterial blood volume -> activation of the RENIN-ANGIOTENSIN-ALDOSTERONE system + ADH -> renal SODIUM and WATER RETENTION -> fluid third-spaces into the peritoneum (worsened by LOW serum albumin reducing oncotic pressure and by portal hypertension). This case centers on the FLOOR management of ascites AND the recognition/treatment of its dangerous infectious complication, SPONTANEOUS BACTERIAL PERITONITIS (SBP) — it is DISTINCT from the acute variceal-hemorrhage emergency in decompensated cirrhosis ([[case-050]]) and from acute liver failure from acetaminophen ([[case-062]]). SBP is infection of pre-existing ascitic fluid WITHOUT a surgical source; presentation is often SUBTLE in a cirrhotic — low-grade fever, diffuse abdominal pain/tenderness, or simply WORSENING ENCEPHALOPATHY, ileus, hypotension, or unexplained renal/clinical decline (a high index of suspicion is required; classic peritoneal rigidity may be ABSENT). DIAGNOSIS is by DIAGNOSTIC PARACENTESIS, which should be done in ANY cirrhotic with ascites who is admitted or who deteriorates, and done BEFORE antibiotics: an ascitic-fluid POLYMORPHONUCLEAR (PMN) count >= 250 cells/mm^3 establishes SBP (send fluid for cell count/differential, culture in blood-culture bottles at the bedside, albumin, total protein). TREATMENT of SBP: empiric IV third-generation CEPHALOSPORIN (cefotaxime 2 g IV q8h, or ceftriaxone) started promptly after the diagnostic tap, PLUS IV ALBUMIN (classically 1.5 g/kg on day 1 and 1 g/kg on day 3) to expand volume and PREVENT HEPATORENAL SYNDROME (HRS) — a key, high-yield, mortality-reducing intervention; avoid nephrotoxins. After an SBP episode, secondary ANTIBIOTIC PROPHYLAXIS (e.g., daily fluoroquinolone or TMP-SMX) is indicated. ASCITES MANAGEMENT (non-SBP, ongoing): DIETARY SODIUM RESTRICTION (~2 g/day = 88 mmol) is the cornerstone; fluid restriction is NOT routine and is reserved for significant HYPONATREMIA (serum Na < ~125 mEq/L). Diuretics = SPIRONOLACTONE (aldosterone antagonist, first-line) combined with FUROSEMIDE in a ~100 mg : 40 mg ratio, titrated to a goal weight loss of ~0.5 kg/day without peripheral edema (faster diuresis risks intravascular depletion -> AKI/HRS). Monitor DAILY WEIGHTS (the best measure of fluid response), abdominal girth, strict I&O, and electrolytes/renal function. LARGE-VOLUME PARACENTESIS for tense or refractory ascites gives symptomatic relief and is paired with IV ALBUMIN (~6-8 g per liter removed when > 5 L) to prevent post-paracentesis circulatory dysfunction. HEPATIC ENCEPHALOPATHY (HE) — driven by gut-derived nitrogenous toxins (ammonia) the failing liver cannot clear; identify and treat PRECIPITANTS (infection/SBP, GI bleed, constipation, dehydration/over-diuresis, electrolyte derangement, sedatives). FIRST-LINE = LACTULOSE titrated to 2-3 SOFT STOOLS per day (it acidifies the gut, traps ammonia as ammonium, and is cathartic) — do NOT withhold it for 'diarrhea' unless excessive; add RIFAXIMIN (a non-absorbed antibiotic) to reduce recurrence. NUTRITION (high yield): cirrhotic patients are catabolic/malnourished and need ADEQUATE PROTEIN (~1.2-1.5 g/kg/day) — the OLD practice of protein restriction in HE is OUTDATED and HARMFUL; provide small frequent meals + a bedtime snack to limit catabolism. AVOID: NSAIDs and aminoglycosides and IV contrast where possible (nephrotoxic -> precipitate HRS), and AVOID benzodiazepines/opioids/sedatives (precipitate or mask HE). Watch for the lethal complications — HRS (rising creatinine/oliguria), variceal bleeding, worsening HE, hyponatremia, and progression to multi-organ failure. The COMFORT/BASIC-CARE focus: relieve dyspnea and abdominal discomfort from tense ascites (semi-Fowler's positioning), meticulous skin care over a distended/edematous abdomen and dependent edema, fall precautions (encephalopathy + diuretics + ascites alter balance), oral care, and emotional support; involve the dietitian, and address ALCOHOL CESSATION with non-judgmental teaching and resources. PERTINENT MISTAKES TO AVOID: starting antibiotics before the diagnostic tap; giving SBP antibiotics WITHOUT albumin; protein-restricting the malnourished cirrhotic; withholding lactulose; over-aggressive diuresis precipitating AKI/HRS; routine fluid restriction when sodium is normal; and giving NSAIDs or sedatives. Step-6 evaluates whether each finding/response reflects IMPROVEMENT, WORSENING, or is UNCHANGED.

patient.name: R. Halvorsen
patient.admitDate: Today, 1010 — admitted to the medical-surgical floor. Dx: decompensated ALCOHOL-RELATED cirrhosis (Child-Pugh C) with tense ascites. PMH: cirrhosis x 3 yrs, two prior paracenteses, esophageal varices (banded), prior hepatic encephalopathy. Presents with ~1 week of worsening abdominal distension and discomfort, decreased appetite, low-grade fever, and 2 days of increasing confusion/sleepiness per his wife. He is drowsy but rousable, with ASTERIXIS and diffuse mild-to-moderate abdominal tenderness. The team performs a DIAGNOSTIC paracentesis: ascitic PMN 480 cells/mm^3 = SPONTANEOUS BACTERIAL PERITONITIS. Plan: empiric IV cefotaxime + IV albumin, sodium-restricted diet with spironolactone + furosemide and daily weights, lactulose titrated to 2-3 soft stools, adequate protein, and avoidance of NSAIDs/nephrotoxins/sedatives.
patient.room: 5-West, Bed 4; medical-surgical floor; wife at bedside; hepatology and dietitian consulted

vitals.spo2: 95% RA; temp 38.3°C (low-grade fever); drowsy but rousable, oriented x2 (misses date), ASTERIXIS present; abdomen distended/tense with a fluid wave and diffuse mild-to-moderate tenderness; 2+ pitting lower-extremity edema

96% RA; temp 38.1°C; diagnostic tap done BEFORE antibiotics — ascitic fluid sent for cell count/differential, culture (blood-culture bottles at bedside), albumin, and total protein; PMN returns 480 cells/mm^3 = SBP; large-volume therapeutic removal paired with IV albumin; reports less abdominal pressure

97% RA; temp 37.9°C; empiric IV cefotaxime started after the tap PLUS IV albumin (1.5 g/kg day 1) to prevent hepatorenal syndrome; spironolactone + furosemide ordered; lactulose started and titrated to 2-3 soft stools/day; NSAIDs and sedatives held; semi-Fowler's for comfort

98% RA; temp 37.2°C; more alert, oriented x3, asterixis decreasing after lactulose produced 3 soft stools; weight down 0.6 kg; abdomen softer/less tense; eating small frequent meals with adequate protein per dietitian; creatinine stable

98% RA; temp 36.9°C; afebrile, alert/oriented, no asterixis; second IV albumin dose (1 g/kg) given; cumulative weight down ~1.4 kg without orthostasis or rising creatinine; appetite improved; abdominal girth decreased

98% RA; temp 36.8°C; SBP treated; started on secondary SBP antibiotic prophylaxis; teaching on 2 g sodium diet, daily weights, lactulose to 2-3 stools, adequate protein, avoiding NSAIDs/sedatives/alcohol; hepatology follow-up + alcohol-cessation resources arranged; transplant-evaluation discussion initiated

labs.n: PRESENTATION + the pathophysiology of cirrhotic ascites and SBP
labs.v: 58 y/o man R. Halvorsen with decompensated alcohol-related cirrhosis, admitted with worsening tense ascites, low-grade fever, diffuse abdominal tenderness, and early hepatic encephalopathy (drowsiness + asterixis). Ascites results from PORTAL HYPERTENSION -> splanchnic vasodilation -> reduced effective arterial volume -> RAAS/ADH activation -> renal Na+/water retention, compounded by LOW albumin (low oncotic pressure). SPONTANEOUS BACTERIAL PERITONITIS = infection of pre-existing ascitic fluid with NO surgical source; presentation in cirrhotics is often SUBTLE (low-grade fever, diffuse tenderness, or just worsening encephalopathy/renal function). The cornerstone: a DIAGNOSTIC PARACENTESIS in any admitted/deteriorating cirrhotic with ascites, done BEFORE antibiotics; ascitic PMN >= 250 cells/mm^3 = SBP. This case is the floor management of ascites + recognition/treatment of SBP — distinct from acute variceal hemorrhage ([[case-050]]) and acetaminophen acute liver failure ([[case-062]]).
labs.r: Cirrhosis -> portal HTN -> splanchnic vasodilation -> RAAS/ADH -> Na+/water retention -> ascites (worsened by low albumin). SBP = infection of ascitic fluid, NO surgical source, often SUBTLE (low-grade fever / diffuse tenderness / worsening HE or renal function). Do a DIAGNOSTIC PARACENTESIS before antibiotics in any admitted/deteriorating cirrhotic with ascites; PMN >= 250 = SBP. RN role: recognize subtle SBP + ensure the diagnostic tap precedes antibiotics.

labs.n: SBP diagnosis + treatment (antibiotics + ALBUMIN to prevent HRS) + prophylaxis
labs.v: DIAGNOSIS: ascitic-fluid PMN >= 250 cells/mm^3 (here 480) establishes SBP regardless of culture; send fluid for cell count/differential, CULTURE in blood-culture bottles at the bedside (higher yield), albumin, and total protein; SBP is typically MONOMICROBIAL (often E. coli/Klebsiella/Streptococcus) — polymicrobial or very high protein suggests SECONDARY (surgical) peritonitis instead. TREATMENT: empiric IV third-generation CEPHALOSPORIN (CEFOTAXIME 2 g IV q8h, or ceftriaxone) started promptly AFTER the diagnostic tap PLUS IV ALBUMIN (1.5 g/kg on day 1 and 1 g/kg on day 3) — albumin expands effective volume and markedly reduces HEPATORENAL SYNDROME and mortality (a high-yield, evidence-based pairing). AVOID nephrotoxins (NSAIDs, aminoglycosides, IV contrast). PROPHYLAXIS: after an SBP episode, give secondary antibiotic prophylaxis (e.g., daily norfloxacin/ciprofloxacin or TMP-SMX); primary prophylaxis is considered for low-ascitic-protein or post-variceal-bleed patients.
labs.r: SBP dx = ascitic PMN >= 250/mm^3 (culture often negative); culture at bedside in blood-culture bottles; usually MONOMICROBIAL (polymicrobial -> think secondary peritonitis). Tx = IV 3rd-gen cephalosporin (cefotaxime/ceftriaxone) AFTER the tap + IV ALBUMIN (1.5 g/kg day 1, 1 g/kg day 3) to prevent HEPATORENAL SYNDROME + death. Avoid nephrotoxins. Give secondary prophylaxis after an episode. RN role: tap before abx, give albumin with abx, monitor renal function.

labs.n: Ascites management — sodium restriction, diuretics, daily weights, paracentesis + albumin
labs.v: DIET: SODIUM restriction ~2 g/day (88 mmol) is the cornerstone of ascites control. FLUID restriction is NOT routine — reserve it for significant HYPONATREMIA (serum Na < ~125 mEq/L). DIURETICS: SPIRONOLACTONE (aldosterone antagonist, FIRST-LINE because ascites is an aldosterone-driven, sodium-retentive state) combined with FUROSEMIDE in a ~100:40 mg ratio (the ratio helps maintain normokalemia — spironolactone retains K+, furosemide wastes it); titrate to a goal of ~0.5 kg/day weight loss (up to ~1 kg/day if peripheral edema is present). MONITORING: DAILY WEIGHTS at the same time/scale/clothing is the BEST measure of fluid response; also abdominal girth, strict I&O, orthostatic vitals, electrolytes, and renal function. Over-aggressive diuresis -> intravascular depletion -> AKI/HRS, hyponatremia, or worsened encephalopathy. LARGE-VOLUME PARACENTESIS for tense or diuretic-refractory ascites relieves dyspnea/discomfort and is paired with IV ALBUMIN ~6-8 g per liter removed when > 5 L to prevent post-paracentesis circulatory dysfunction; position upright, empty bladder first, monitor for hypotension afterward, and assess the site for leakage/infection.
labs.r: Ascites: 2 g/day SODIUM restriction (cornerstone); fluid restriction ONLY if Na < ~125. Diuretics = SPIRONOLACTONE first-line + FUROSEMIDE (~100:40 ratio keeps K+ balanced); titrate to ~0.5 kg/day loss. DAILY WEIGHTS = best fluid-response measure (+ girth, I&O, lytes, renal). Over-diuresis -> AKI/HRS/hyponatremia/HE. Large-volume paracentesis for tense/refractory ascites + IV albumin 6-8 g/L removed (>5 L). RN role: daily weights, titrated diuresis, post-tap hypotension monitoring.

labs.n: Hepatic encephalopathy (lactulose + rifaximin + precipitants), NUTRITION, drugs to AVOID, + Pertinent NEGATIVES
labs.v: HEPATIC ENCEPHALOPATHY (HE): gut-derived nitrogenous toxins (ammonia) the failing liver cannot clear -> altered mentation, asterixis (flapping tremor), sleep-wake reversal. Always seek and treat a PRECIPITANT: infection/SBP, GI bleed, CONSTIPATION, dehydration/over-diuresis, electrolyte derangement (hypokalemia/alkalosis), or SEDATIVES. FIRST-LINE = LACTULOSE titrated to 2-3 SOFT STOOLS/day (acidifies the colon, traps ammonia as ammonium, cathartic) PO or by retention enema if obtunded; do NOT withhold for mild diarrhea unless excessive (overuse -> dehydration/hypokalemia -> worse HE). Add RIFAXIMIN (non-absorbed antibiotic) to reduce recurrence. NUTRITION (high yield): cirrhotics are catabolic/malnourished and need ADEQUATE PROTEIN (~1.2-1.5 g/kg/day) plus small frequent meals + a bedtime snack — the OLD practice of PROTEIN RESTRICTION for HE is OUTDATED and HARMFUL; involve a dietitian; thiamine/folate/multivitamin for the alcohol-related patient. AVOID: NSAIDs/aminoglycosides/IV contrast (nephrotoxic -> HRS) and benzodiazepines/opioids/sedatives (precipitate/mask HE); acetaminophen is acceptable at REDUCED dose (<= 2 g/day). COMFORT/BASIC CARE: semi-Fowler's for ascites-related dyspnea, meticulous skin care over the distended/edematous abdomen + dependent edema, fall precautions (HE + diuretics + ascites), oral care, emotional support, and non-judgmental ALCOHOL-CESSATION teaching/resources. PERTINENT NEGATIVES (correct practice): did NOT start antibiotics before the diagnostic tap; did NOT give SBP antibiotics WITHOUT albumin; did NOT protein-restrict; did NOT withhold lactulose; did NOT diurese over-aggressively; did NOT fluid-restrict with a normal sodium; did NOT give NSAIDs or sedatives. PROTECTIVE FACTORS: early SBP recognition + tap-before-antibiotics + albumin, titrated diuresis with daily weights, lactulose to target stools, adequate nutrition, nephrotoxin/sedative avoidance, and alcohol-cessation planning.
labs.r: HE = ammonia/nitrogenous toxins -> altered mentation + asterixis; ALWAYS treat a precipitant (SBP, GI bleed, CONSTIPATION, over-diuresis, hypokalemia, sedatives). First-line LACTULOSE to 2-3 SOFT stools/day (don't withhold); add RIFAXIMIN. NUTRITION = ADEQUATE PROTEIN 1.2-1.5 g/kg/day + bedtime snack (protein restriction is OUTDATED/harmful). AVOID NSAIDs/nephrotoxins + benzodiazepines/opioids/sedatives; acetaminophen <= 2 g/day OK. Negatives confirm correct practice (tap before abx, abx WITH albumin, no protein restriction, don't withhold lactulose, no over-diuresis). RN role: lactulose titration + nutrition + nephrotoxin/sedative avoidance.

labs.v: 129 mEq/L (dilutional hyponatremia of cirrhosis; not low enough to mandate fluid restriction)
4.4 mEq/L (normal; monitor closely with spironolactone + furosemide)
1.3 mg/dL (mildly elevated; watch for rise signaling hepatorenal syndrome — albumin given to protect renal function)
2.4 g/dL (low; reduced oncotic pressure contributes to ascites)
1.8 (elevated; impaired hepatic synthesis of clotting factors — reflects synthetic dysfunction, not anticoagulation)
92 x10^9/L (thrombocytopenia from splenic sequestration in portal hypertension)
92 mcmol/L (elevated; supports hepatic encephalopathy — trend clinically, not as a sole treatment target)

nursesNote: 1015 — Med-surg admission. 58 y/o man, decompensated alcohol-related cirrhosis (Child-Pugh C), with ~1 week of worsening abdominal distension + low-grade fever + 2 days of increasing confusion. T 38.3, HR 104, BP 98/58, RR 20, SpO2 95% RA. Drowsy but rousable, oriented x2, ASTERIXIS present. Abdomen tense/distended with fluid wave and diffuse mild-moderate tenderness; 2+ LE edema. SBP suspected given fever + tenderness + worsening encephalopathy in a cirrhotic with ascites. DIAGNOSTIC PARACENTESIS performed BEFORE antibiotics — fluid sent for cell count/differential, culture (blood-culture bottles at bedside), albumin, total protein; PMN 480 = SBP. Empiric IV CEFOTAXIME started after the tap PLUS IV ALBUMIN (1.5 g/kg day 1) to prevent hepatorenal syndrome. Ascites plan: 2 g sodium diet (NO routine fluid restriction — Na 129, not < 125), SPIRONOLACTONE + FUROSEMIDE (~100:40), DAILY WEIGHTS + girth + strict I&O + electrolytes/renal, titrate to ~0.5 kg/day loss. LACTULOSE titrated to 2-3 soft stools/day for HE; precipitant (SBP) treated; RIFAXIMIN added. NUTRITION: ADEQUATE protein (dietitian) + small frequent meals + bedtime snack — NO protein restriction; thiamine/folate/MVI. AVOIDING NSAIDs/nephrotoxins/IV contrast and benzodiazepines/opioids/sedatives. Comfort: semi-Fowler's for ascites dyspnea, skin care over distended abdomen + edema, fall precautions, oral care. Monitoring for HRS (rising creatinine/oliguria), variceal bleed, worsening HE, hyponatremia. Non-judgmental alcohol-cessation teaching + resources; hepatology + transplant-eval discussion initiated. Wife at bedside and updated.
providerOrders: ADMIT: medical-surgical floor; diagnosis decompensated cirrhosis with tense ascites and suspected SBP; vital signs q4h with neuro/mental-status checks

DIAGNOSTIC + THERAPEUTIC PARACENTESIS: obtain ascitic fluid BEFORE antibiotics — cell count with differential, culture in blood-culture bottles at bedside, albumin, total protein; large-volume removal for symptom relief
IV ALBUMIN: 25% albumin with SBP treatment — 1.5 g/kg on day 1 and 1 g/kg on day 3 to prevent hepatorenal syndrome; 6-8 g per liter of ascites removed when large-volume paracentesis exceeds 5 L

ANTIBIOTICS: cefotaxime 2 g IV q8h (third-generation cephalosporin) empiric for SBP, started AFTER the diagnostic tap; de-escalate per culture; begin secondary SBP prophylaxis (daily fluoroquinolone or TMP-SMX) after the acute episode

DIET: 2 g (2000 mg) sodium-restricted diet; NO routine fluid restriction (reserve for serum Na < ~125 mEq/L); dietitian consult for ADEQUATE protein (~1.2-1.5 g/kg/day), small frequent meals + bedtime snack; thiamine, folate, multivitamin
DIURETICS: spironolactone 100 mg PO daily + furosemide 40 mg PO daily (~100:40 ratio); titrate to ~0.5 kg/day weight loss; hold/adjust for AKI, hyponatremia, or hypotension
LACTULOSE: titrate dose to 2-3 soft bowel movements per day for hepatic encephalopathy; rifaximin 550 mg PO BID; identify/treat precipitants

MONITORING: DAILY WEIGHTS (same time/scale/clothing), abdominal girth, strict I&O, orthostatic vitals; daily CMP (sodium, potassium, creatinine), CBC; assess for hepatorenal syndrome, variceal bleeding, and worsening encephalopathy
AVOID: NSAIDs, aminoglycosides, and IV contrast (nephrotoxic); benzodiazepines, opioids, and other sedatives (precipitate/mask encephalopathy); acetaminophen if used <= 2 g/day

SAFETY/COMFORT: fall precautions; semi-Fowler's positioning for ascites-related dyspnea; meticulous skin care over distended abdomen and dependent edema; oral care; alcohol-cessation counseling/resources; hepatology and transplant-evaluation referral

steps.title: Recognize Cues
steps.sub: What matters in this decompensated cirrhotic?

opts.text: Low-grade fever (38.3°C) + diffuse abdominal tenderness + WORSENING confusion in a cirrhotic with ascites — a subtle presentation of SBP
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — SBP in cirrhotics is often subtle; fever, diffuse tenderness, or new/worsening encephalopathy should trigger a diagnostic paracentesis.

opts.text: Tense, distended abdomen with a fluid wave and 2+ lower-extremity edema
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — tense ascites + edema reflects sodium/water retention from portal hypertension and low albumin; needs diuresis and, if symptomatic, paracentesis.

opts.text: Drowsiness, disorientation, and ASTERIXIS (flapping tremor)
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — classic hepatic encephalopathy; here precipitated by SBP. Treat the precipitant and start lactulose.

opts.text: Ascitic-fluid PMN count of 480 cells/mm^3
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — PMN >= 250 is diagnostic of SBP regardless of culture and mandates prompt antibiotics + albumin.

opts.text: Borderline-low BP (98/58) with mild creatinine elevation (1.3 mg/dL)
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — reduced effective arterial volume; the rising creatinine raises concern for hepatorenal syndrome, which IV albumin helps prevent.

opts.text: Serum sodium 129 mEq/L
opts.rat: RELEVANT — dilutional hyponatremia of cirrhosis; important for diuretic decisions, but NOT low enough (< ~125) to require routine fluid restriction.

opts.text: The elevated INR (1.8) means he should immediately receive vitamin K and FFP to reverse 'anticoagulation' before any care
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG. The INR is elevated from impaired hepatic synthesis, NOT anticoagulation; routine reversal is not indicated absent active bleeding/procedure need.

opts.text: His confusion is expected for his age and needs no workup
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG. New/worsening confusion in a cirrhotic is encephalopathy until proven otherwise and signals a precipitant (here, SBP) — it requires active workup and treatment.

opts.text: Ascites results from portal hypertension -> splanchnic vasodilation -> RAAS/ADH activation -> renal sodium and water retention, worsened by low oncotic pressure from hypoalbuminemia
opts.rat: CORRECT — the pathophysiologic chain that justifies sodium restriction + diuretics targeting aldosterone.

opts.text: An ascitic PMN >= 250 cells/mm^3 establishes SBP even if the culture is ultimately negative
opts.rat: CORRECT — culture-negative neutrocytic ascites is still treated as SBP; the PMN count drives the diagnosis.

opts.text: SBP can precipitate hepatic encephalopathy and hepatorenal syndrome — so treating the infection (with antibiotics AND albumin) also treats the encephalopathy and protects the kidneys
opts.rat: CORRECT — infection is a common HE precipitant; albumin reduces HRS and mortality in SBP.

opts.text: Spironolactone is first-line for cirrhotic ascites because the state is aldosterone-driven; pairing it with furosemide in a ~100:40 ratio helps maintain potassium balance
opts.rat: CORRECT — the rationale for the combination and ratio.

opts.text: Hepatic encephalopathy is driven by gut-derived nitrogenous toxins (ammonia); lactulose lowers gut ammonia and is titrated to 2-3 soft stools per day
opts.rat: CORRECT — mechanism and dosing target of first-line therapy.

opts.text: Because he is encephalopathic, dietary protein should be strictly restricted to lower ammonia
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG. Protein restriction is OUTDATED and HARMFUL; malnourished cirrhotics need ADEQUATE protein (~1.2-1.5 g/kg/day). Lactulose/rifaximin manage ammonia.

opts.text: His normal-range potassium and mild hyponatremia mean diuretics carry no risk and can be pushed aggressively for rapid fluid removal
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG. Over-aggressive diuresis causes intravascular depletion -> AKI/HRS, worsening hyponatremia, and encephalopathy; titrate to ~0.5 kg/day.

opts.text: Diagnose and treat SBP — diagnostic paracentesis BEFORE antibiotics, then prompt IV cephalosporin PLUS IV albumin
opts.rat: #1 — HIGHEST. Untreated SBP is rapidly fatal and drives the encephalopathy and renal risk; the tap must precede antibiotics and albumin must accompany them.

opts.text: Treat hepatic encephalopathy by correcting the precipitant and starting lactulose titrated to 2-3 soft stools/day (and protect the airway/safety while confused)
opts.rat: #2 — HIGH. HE affects mentation/safety; treating the SBP precipitant plus lactulose reverses it.

opts.text: Protect renal function — give albumin, monitor creatinine/urine output, and avoid nephrotoxins to prevent hepatorenal syndrome
opts.rat: #3 — HRS carries very high mortality; albumin + nephrotoxin avoidance are preventive priorities running alongside SBP care.

opts.text: Manage the ascites itself — sodium restriction, titrated spironolactone + furosemide, daily weights, and paracentesis for symptomatic relief
opts.rat: #4 — Important ongoing management, addressed in parallel but secondary to treating the acute infection and its complications.

opts.text: Provide nutrition optimization and alcohol-cessation/discharge teaching
opts.rat: #5 — Essential for long-term outcome but lower acuity than the acute infection, mentation, and renal threats.

steps.title: Generate Solutions
steps.inst: For EACH action, classify as INDICATED or NOT INDICATED.

opts.text: Perform a diagnostic paracentesis (cell count/differential, culture in blood-culture bottles, albumin, protein) BEFORE giving antibiotics
opts.rat: The tap establishes the diagnosis and preserves culture yield; it must precede antibiotics.

opts.text: Start empiric IV cefotaxime (third-generation cephalosporin) PLUS IV albumin after the tap
opts.rat: Standard SBP therapy; albumin reduces hepatorenal syndrome and mortality.

opts.text: Order a 2 g sodium diet and start spironolactone + furosemide titrated with daily weights
opts.rat: Sodium restriction + aldosterone-antagonist-based diuresis is the cornerstone of ascites control; daily weights gauge response.

opts.text: Start lactulose titrated to 2-3 soft stools/day and add rifaximin for the encephalopathy
opts.rat: First-line HE therapy; rifaximin reduces recurrence.

opts.text: Provide adequate dietary protein (~1.2-1.5 g/kg/day) with small frequent meals and a bedtime snack
opts.rat: Cirrhotics are catabolic; adequate protein supports recovery. Protein restriction is outdated/harmful.

opts.text: Pair large-volume paracentesis with IV albumin (~6-8 g per liter removed when > 5 L) and monitor for post-tap hypotension
opts.rat: Albumin prevents post-paracentesis circulatory dysfunction during large-volume removal.

opts.text: Give SBP antibiotics but omit albumin to avoid 'fluid overload'
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG — withholding albumin in SBP increases hepatorenal syndrome and mortality; albumin is part of standard therapy.

opts.text: Strictly restrict dietary protein to lower his ammonia level
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG — protein restriction worsens malnutrition without benefit; manage ammonia with lactulose/rifaximin.

opts.text: Give ibuprofen for his abdominal discomfort and lorazepam for restlessness
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG — NSAIDs are nephrotoxic (precipitate HRS) and benzodiazepines precipitate/mask encephalopathy; both are contraindicated.

opts.text: Impose a strict 1000 mL/day fluid restriction now because he has ascites
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG — fluid restriction is not routine; it is reserved for significant hyponatremia (Na < ~125). His Na is 129.

steps.title: Take Action
steps.inst: Rank the order of actions. #1 = FIRST.

opts.text: Facilitate/assist the diagnostic paracentesis and send the ascitic fluid studies BEFORE antibiotics are given
opts.rat: #1 — FIRST. Securing the diagnosis (and culture yield) before antibiotics is the pivotal early step.

opts.text: Administer empiric IV cefotaxime plus IV albumin promptly after the tap
opts.rat: #2 — Second. Prompt antibiotics + albumin treat the infection and protect the kidneys.

opts.text: Start lactulose titrated to 2-3 soft stools/day and institute fall/aspiration safety precautions for the confused patient
opts.rat: #3 — Third. Treats encephalopathy and keeps the patient safe while mentation is impaired.

opts.text: Initiate the 2 g sodium diet with spironolactone + furosemide and begin daily weights, girth, and strict I&O
opts.rat: #4 — Fourth. Ongoing ascites management with the key monitoring parameters.

opts.text: Provide nutrition (adequate protein) and non-judgmental alcohol-cessation teaching, and arrange hepatology/transplant follow-up
opts.rat: #5 — Closing. Optimizes long-term outcome and prevents recurrence.

steps.title: Evaluate Outcomes
steps.inst: Classify each finding as the patient IMPROVING, WORSENING, or UNCHANGED.

opts.text: By Day 2 he is afebrile-trending, oriented x3, and asterixis is decreasing after lactulose produced 3 soft stools
opts.rat: Resolving infection and encephalopathy — the SBP precipitant is being controlled and ammonia is clearing.

opts.text: Weight is down ~1.4 kg over 3 days without orthostasis and with a stable creatinine
opts.rat: Goal-rate diuresis (~0.5 kg/day) achieving fluid loss WITHOUT intravascular depletion or renal injury.

opts.text: Abdominal girth is decreasing and he reports easier breathing in semi-Fowler's
opts.rat: Ascites and the associated dyspnea/discomfort are responding to therapy and positioning.

opts.text: Urine output falls to 15 mL/hr and creatinine rises from 1.3 to 2.4 mg/dL despite a normal blood pressure
opts.rat: ⚠️ Signals hepatorenal syndrome / AKI — hold diuretics, ensure albumin/volume, avoid nephrotoxins, and notify the provider urgently.

opts.text: He becomes more somnolent with increased asterixis and has had no bowel movement for 24 hours
opts.rat: ⚠️ Worsening encephalopathy with constipation as a precipitant — increase lactulose to achieve 2-3 stools and re-evaluate for new precipitants.

opts.text: He vomits bright-red blood and his heart rate climbs to 120 with BP 86/50
opts.rat: ⚠️ Variceal hemorrhage / hypovolemic shock — a life-threatening complication of portal hypertension requiring emergent resuscitation ([[case-050]]).

opts.text: Serum sodium drifts from 129 to 124 mEq/L after several days of diuretics
opts.rat: ⚠️ Worsening hyponatremia now in the range that warrants fluid restriction and diuretic reassessment to avoid further drop.

opts.text: His potassium is 4.3 mEq/L and unchanged from admission on the spironolactone/furosemide combination
opts.rat: Stable potassium reflects the balanced 100:40 spironolactone-to-furosemide ratio working as intended.

opts.text: He remains afebrile with a soft, non-tender abdomen at discharge and is started on secondary SBP prophylaxis
opts.rat: SBP resolved; secondary prophylaxis reduces recurrence risk — the desired trajectory.
